coredata.dc:title: Improving mango shelf-life using PVA composite films prepared with Zn-doped carbon quantum dots derived from crayfish shells

coredata.dc:description: Food packaging plays a vital role in preserving various agricultural and food commodities for postharvest management. This study aimed to investigate postharvest preservation technologies for mangoes using composite films made from polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and Zn-doped carbon quantum dots (Zn-CQDs). The results showed a marked increase in the antibacterial potential of the composite films after Zn doping. Furthermore, the addition of Zn-CQDs significantly improved the mechanical properties, thermal stability and ultraviolet absorption of the Zn-CQDs/PVA composite films. In addition, the mango preservation experiment demonstrated that the 5% Zn-CQDs/PVA composite film was effective in delaying the decline in mango hardness (the composite film group was 63.2% higher than the control), VC content (the composite film group was 27.5% higher than the control), titratable acid content (the composite film group was 149% higher than the control), and the incline of disease index (the composite film group was 23.1% lower than the control), total soluble solids (the composite film group was 27.3% lower than the control), and weight loss rate (the composite film group was 25.7% lower than the control). These findings suggest that the Zn-CQDs/PVA composite films hold great potential as a new type of active packaging in the food industry.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Food packaging plays a vital role in preserving various agricultural and food commodities for postharvest management. This study aimed to investigate postharvest preservation technologies for mangoes using composite films made from polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and Zn-doped carbon quantum dots (Zn-CQDs). The results showed a marked increase in the antibacterial potential of the composite films after Zn doping. Furthermore, the addition of Zn-CQDs significantly improved the mechanical properties, thermal stability and ultraviolet absorption of the Zn-CQDs/PVA composite films. In addition, the mango preservation experiment demonstrated that the 5% Zn-CQDs/PVA composite film was effective in delaying the decline in mango hardness (the composite film group was 63.2% higher than the control), VC content (the composite film group was 27.5% higher than the control), titratable acid content (the composite film group was 149% higher than the control), and the incline of disease index (the composite film group was 23.1% lower than the control), total soluble solids (the composite film group was 27.3% lower than the control), and weight loss rate (the composite film group was 25.7% lower than the control). These findings suggest that the Zn-CQDs/PVA composite films hold great potential as a new type of active packaging in the food industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Mango, often acclaimed as the ‘king of tropical fruits’, is adored globally for its delightful flavor, delectable taste, and abundant nutritional benefits (W. Zhou et al., 2021). Despite their popularity, mangoes are susceptible to decay due to their strong metabolism after harvesting and the invasion of microorganisms during the long transit and storage periods, resulting in enormous economic losses and wastage of resources annually (Wang et al., 2022). Consequently, postharvest preservation technologies for mangoes have attracted considerable attention.

Food packaging plays an essential role in postharvest preservation of numerous agricultural and food products. olystyrene, polyethylene terephthalate, polyvinyl chloride, and polypropylene are extensively employed in the realm of food packaging (Zhang et al., 2022). However, environmental pollution caused by non-biodegradable petroleum-based plastic materials cannot be disregarded. Moreover, traditional food packagings without any modifications offer restricted assistance in preserving food quality and provides limited defence against external physical damage and pollution. To address these limitations, active food packaging films have recently garnered considerable attention from the food industry. Unlike traditional packaging, active packaging incorporates preservation technology by adding antioxidants or antimicrobials to the packaging material, thereby maintaining food quality and extending the shelf life of food products (Yousuf, Qadri, & Srivastava, 2018).

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) emerges as a polymer with several advantageous properties, such as biodegradability, biocompatibility, low toxicity, excellent film-forming ability, and mechanical strength, making it highly suitable for various applications, including drug release, film preparation, and food packaging (Tripathi, Mehrotra, & Dutta, 2010). Numerous active substances have been incorporated into PVA to prepare PVA-active food-packaging films. For example, Roy et al. incorporated PVA with grapefruit seed extract and curcumin to improve the antioxidant properties (DPPH and ABTS) and antimicrobial ability (Roy & Rhim, 2021). Similarly, Lin et al. prepared a composite film from PVA and graphene oxide (GO) by incorporating oregano oil, which significantly enhanced the inhibition ability against Escherichia coli (E. coli) compared to the essential oil-free film (D. Lin et al., 2019). Gaikwad et al. designed a PVA film containing apple pomace (AP), and their findings demonstrated that the DPPH scavenging activity of PVA/AP films increased with increasing AP content (Gaikwad, Lee, & Lee, 2016).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a novel category of fluorescent nanomaterials since 2004 (X. Xu et al., 2004) and possess various exceptional properties such as photoluminescence, stable photostability, low toxicity, and high biocapacity (Gao et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2022). In recent years, researchers have increasingly focused on their antibacterial and antioxidant properties, as well as their potential applications in the food industry. CQDs synthesized using different precursors have been reported to exhibit antibacterial properties. Li et al. summarized the antibacterial mechanism of CQDs, where CQDs were adsorbed onto cell walls, damaged the cell membrane, and finally resulted in cellular cytoplasmic leakage. Moreover, CQDs have been observed to penetrate cellular walls and membranes, thereby facilitating the binding of DNA and RNA molecules, consequently inducing alterations in bacterial and fungal structures (P. Li et al., 2022). Surendran et al. utilized natural honey to synthesize antibacterial CQDs, which showed antimicrobial activity against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and E. coli (Surendran et al., 2020). In addition, CQDs have been found to exhibit superior antioxidant and antimicrobial properties compared to common agents, such as polyphenols and essential oils. For example, studies on the synthesis of CQDs from animal and plant by-products, such as mango peel and eggshells, using hydrothermal synthesis and microwave methods, are also increasingly common (H. Fan, Zhang, Bhandari, & Yang, 2020). These advantages make CQDs a promising functional nano-additive in food packaging films (Hongying Du & He, 2024).

The present study focused on the preparation of Zinc-doped carbon quantum dots (Zn-CQDs) and their antibacterial ability was enhanced. Subsequently, novel biodegradable Zn-CQDs-PVA composite films were successfully prepared by grafting PVA and Zn-CQDs synthesized from crayfish shells. The thickness, mechanical properties, color properties, ultraviolet–visible absorption and transmittance, water properties, and thermal stability of the newly formed composite films were evaluated. Additionally, the quality changes of the control group mangoes, mangoes coated with Zn-CQDs-PVA composite film, and PVA film were investigated and compared. The composite film is conducive to improving food packaging materials and could be effectively utilized within food packaging industry.

Crayfish and mango were purchased from a local supermarket in Wuhan, China. PVA powder 1799 was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The remaining reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). S. aureus (CMCC26003) and E. coli (CMCC44102) strains were procured from Qingdao Hope Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Qingdao, China).

Initially, a solution of zinc acetate was obtained by dissolving 0.548 g of zinc acetate dihydrate in 25 mL of water. The crayfish shells are cleaned, dried, ground, and dispersed into the solution were dispersed in the solution (∼1 g), which was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained in an air oven at hours at 180 °C for 5.5 h. After air cooling, the resultant brownish solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 30 min and filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filters to remove sediments. Subsequently, the filtrate was dialyzed using 1000 D dialysis membranes for 24 h to remove residual impurities. Finally, the solution was lyophilized for 24 h to obtain solid Zn-CQDs.

The Zn-CQDs-PVA composite films were prepared by dissolving PVA (5 g, 5% of the final solution) in 95 mL of deionized water. This mixture was subjected to constant magnetic stirring for 1.5 h at 90 °C. Subsequently, glycerol (1 g) was incorporated into the solution, followed by the addition of varying concentrations (0, 1, 3, and 5% relative concentration of solid PVA) of Zn-CQDs solution (5 mL) to the PVA solution. The resulting mixture was stirred for 10 min to obtain the film-forming solution. The obtained solution was then poured into a plastic plate (diameter 9 cm) and left to dry for 30 h at 25 °C. This process produced Zn-CQDs-PVA composite film.

High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) images of the Zn-CQDs were taken using a Transmission Electron Microscope (JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan) at a voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were conducted to examine the chemical bonds and compositions of the Zn-CQDs using a Kα XPS Analysis Instrument (Thermo Scientific, USA). The fluorescence spectra of Zn-CQDs were recorded using a fluorescence spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan) and the UV–visible absorption spectrum was obtained using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV2600A, Unico, China). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were obtained in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 using a Nicolet iS 50 FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) to identify the functionalities of the Zn-CQDs. The antibacterial activity of the CQDs against E. coli and S. aureus was evaluated using microdilution method with some modification, and the OD value at 600 nm was used to reflect the changes in the number of bacteria (X. Yang, Lan, & Xie, 2022)

The thickness of the Zn-CQDs-PVA composite film was measured using a digital micrometer (PosiTector 6000, DeFelsko Corporation, USA) with a precision of 0.001 mm. Five random points were selected for the measurement, and the average value was reported. The tensile strength (TS, MPa) and elongation at break (EB, %) of the film samples were determined using a Texture Analyzer (TA.XT.plus, Stable Micro Systems, UK) equipped with an A/TG detector.

The color attributes of Zn-CQDs-PVA composite film were assessed using a Colorimeter (Shang Guang WSC-S, Shanghai, China) with measurements recorded as L* (brightness), a*(redness-greenness), and b*(yellowness-blueness). The total color difference (ΔE) was calculated using the following equation (Mao et al., 2023):
(1)

where ΔL* represents the difference in L* values between the standard colored plate and film. Δa* and Δb* denote the a* and b* difference between values of the standard color plate and film, respectively.

The ultraviolet–visible absorption and transmittance of the composite film samples were determined using an ultraviolet–visible light spectrophotometer. To test these characteristics, the film was cut into 1 × 4 cm2 rectangular film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Moisture content, water solubility, water vapor permeability and water contact angle
ce:section.ce:para:
Moisture content and water solubility tests were performed as previously reported with some modifications (H. Du et al., 2021). Film samples (3 × 3 cm2) were weighed (W
i ) and dried in an oven at 105 °C until a constant weight was obtained (W
f ). Moisture content (MS) was calculated using the following equation:
(2)
M S
( % )

W i
−
W f

× 100 %

Water solubility (WS) tests were also performed with some modifications. Film samples (3 × 3 cm2) were weighed (W
i ) and immersed in distilled water for 24 h. The remaining parts of the films were filtered and dried to a constant dry weight (W
f ). The WS was calculated using the following equation:
(3)
W S
( % )
=
W i
−
W f
W i
× 100 %

The water vapor permeability test was conducted as previously reported method with some modification (Khoshgozaran-Abras, Azizi, Hamidy, & Bagheripoor-Fallah, 2012). A plastic cup with dimensions of 4.5 cm (depth) × 3.2 cm (diameter) was filled with 10 mL of distilled water. The film was then placed over the cup opening and tied tightly using a rubber band. Subsequently, the cups were placed in a desiccator containing silica gel to maintain a 0% relative humidity at 25 °C. Changes in cup weight were recorded after 24 h. The measured WVP (g⋅m/m2⋅h⋅Pa) was calculated using the following equation:
(4)
W V P =
Δ m × d
A × Δ p × t
Where Δm is the change values of cups weights (g); d and A are the thickness of film (m) and film area (m
2 ); Δp is the partial vapor pressure difference between the two sides of the film (3179 Pa); and t is the test period (24 h).
The values and images of the water contact angles of the composite films were obtained using a LSA100 contact Angle Meter (LAUDA Scientific, Germany) with a drop of distilled water (5 μL).

Thermogravimetric analysis of the composite film was performed using a NETZSCH TG 209F1 Libra thermogravimetric analyzer (NETZSCH, Germany). The sample was tested in a nitrogen atmosphere at a flow rate of 25 mL/min from an initial temperature of 30 °C to a final temperature of 700 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of the film was performed using a Nicolet iS 50 fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). Attenuated Total Reflectance (ATR) was used for the direct analysis of the films. The testing range for the spectra was 4000 cm−1 to 500 cm−1.

The cross-sectional microstructure of the composite film was examined using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, HITACHI SU80). A 1 × 1 cm sample of the composite film was prepared by cutting the surface smoothly with scissors to obtain a cross section, which was then observed using a scanning electron microscope. The acceleration voltage of the instrument was set at 3 kV, lens height was adjusted between 7.7 and 10 mm, and magnification was 10 k. The test mode was set to SE (UL).

The antioxidant activity of the composite films was assessed using the two methods: DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) and ABTS (2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) free radical scavenging assays, with slight modifications based on former methods (Jamroz et al., 2019; Kousheh, Moradi, Tajik, & Molaei, 2020).

For the DPPH scavenging assay, 0.1 g of film samples were added to 4 mL of ethyl alcohol. After 6 h, DPPH was added to the mixture to obtain a final concentration of 0.1 mM. The mixture was incubated in the dark for 30 min. Subsequently, the absorbance of the DPPH solution (A
0 ) and the sample mixture solution (A
i ) was measured at 517 nm. The percentage of DPPH free radical-scavenging activity was then calculated using the following equation:
(5)

The mangoes were randomly divided into three groups, each comprising 150 mangoes of the same maturity and size, and 50 were used for the disease index test. The control group was immersed in distilled water, while the PVA and Zn-CQDs groups were immersed in the PVA and Zn-CQDs-PVA film solutions for 1 min, respectively. Subsequently, mangoes were dried at room temperature for film formation. The mangoes were stored at room temperature (25 ± 2 °C) for 10 days, and the quality index was measured on days 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10. The coating method enables the film to be closer to the mango. Meanwhile, the improvement due to carbon quantum dots also appear in the coating film.

The disease index of the mango fruits was calculated using the following procedure: the mangoes were divided into five grades based on the area of the black spots on the surface. Grade 0 signified no decay; grade 1, 1–10% black spots; grade 2, 11–20% black spots; grade 3, 20–50% black spots; and grade 4, ≥50% black spots. The disease index was calculated using the following formula:

The hardness of the mango samples was determined using a wooden house-type hardness tester (MF32H043019-AF, Shiro, Japan), and the values were expressed in Newtons (N).

To determine the vitamin C content in the mango juice samples, 50 mg of 2,6-dichlorindophenol sodium was dissolved in 200 mL of distilled water at 65 °C. Subsequently, 52 mg sodium bicarbonate was added to the solution, which was then cooled at 4 °C for 12 h. Then, the 2,6-dichlor indophenol sodium solution was demarcated with ascorbic acid and designated for further use. Five mango fruits (10 g) were used to prepare mango juice samples with 2% oxalic acid (10 mL) and centrifuged at 6000 RPM for 15 min to obtain the supernatant. Finally, the supernatant was titrated with 2,6-dichlor indophenol sodium. The VC content was calculated as follows:

0 (mL) represents the volume of dye solution for the titration of deionized water. m (10 g) represents the total weight of mango sample.

Mango juice was prepared by homogenizing and filtering 10 g of mango fruit sourced five mangoes, followed by dilution with deionized water to achieve a final volume of 100 mL. After standing for 30 min, 10 mL of mango juice solution was titrated with NaOH (0.1 M) solution using phenolphthalein as the indicator. The titratable acidity was calculated using the following equation:

t (10 mL) is the volume of mango juice for titration, and V (100 mL) is the total volume of the mango juice solution. The total weight of the mango sample (10 g) is denoted as m.
The TSS of the mango fruits was determined using a portable refractometer (HT113ATC, YHT, China). The juice from the five mangoes was mixed and coarse-filtered prior to testing.

The gravimetric method was used to determine the weight loss of mango fruits. Specifically, the weight of mango fruits (five specified fruits per group) was recorded every alternate day. The percentage of weight loss was calculated using the following equation:

The figures were drawn using Origin 2022 (OriginLab, USA). The statistical analyses were performed using Microsoft Excel (Microsoft, USA) and SPSS 22.0 (IBM, USA) and the significant differences between the means (P < 0.05) were determined by one-way ANOVA test. For all the studies, the values were presented as mean ± standard deviation (n ≥ 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphology and size of the Zn-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the Zn-CQDs was analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM images show that the synthesized Zn-CQDs were predominantly spherical and evenly dispersed in water (Fig. 1 a). A particle size distribution diagram illustrated that the particle sizes of the Zn-CQDs ranged from 2 to 6 nm, with an average particle size of 4.01 nm (based on 100 randomly selected particles) (Fig. 1b). Das et al. also designed zinc-doped CQDs using citric acid and tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane as precursors via a hydrothermal method. The Zn-doped CQDs exhibited similar average diameter (4.5 nm) (P. Das et al., 2018).

The optical properties of Zn-CQDs were characterized using fluorescence and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra. As illustrated in Fig. 1c, the maximum emission peak of the Zn-CQDs was observed at 462 nm under the optimal excitation wavelength of 360 nm. In the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1c), the peaks at 265 and 335 nm correspond to the n→π* transition of the C=O double bonds and π→π* transition of the aromatic ring, respectively. Similar results were obtained for the preparation of chitosan/thiourea/citric acid CQDs in a previous study (Chaghaghazardi et al., 2023). Furthermore, Fig. 1d displays the fluorescence emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths, showing a red-shift in the emission peaks with an excitation wavelength ranging from 300 to 400 nm. This excitation-dependent phenomenon can be ascribed to different particle sizes and considerable surface defects resulting from the presence of abundant functional groups (Raja et al., 2021).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze and determine the chemical composition of the Zn-CQDs and to confirm the successful doping of Zn. The XPS spectra (Fig. 1e) showed peaks at 1052.73, 532.09, and 284.8 eV, which corresponded to Zn2p, N1s, and C1s, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) exhibited three peaks at 284.8, 286.5, and 289.0 eV, which represent four carbon bonds: C=C/C-C, C-O, and COO-, respectively. The deconvoluted O1s spectrum (Fig. 1g) contained two peaks at 531.39 and 533.23 eV, which were attributed to C=O/Zn-O, and C-OH/C-O-C, respectively. The high-resolution Zn2p spectra (Fig. 1h) comprised of the Zn-2p2/3(1022.13 eV) and Zn-2p1/2(1045.22 eV) core levels of Zn-CQDs. Thus, the XPS and FT-IR analyses confirmed the presence of abundant functional groups, such as -OH and -COOH, on the Zn-CQDs and demonstrated that zinc was successfully doped.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial ability of Zn-CQDs and crayfish shells-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1i and j, both crayfish shells-CQDs (CS-CQDs, without zinc doping) and Zn-CQDs exhibited antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus. The OD value reflects the bacterial count. Notably, the Zn-CQDs treated groups generally exhibited lower OD values at various concentrations, indicating that Zn-CQDs possess stronger antibacterial properties than the CS-CQDs. Significant differences were observed when the CQDs concentrations were 0.07813 mg/mL and 0.03906 mg/mL, respectively, which indicates that zinc doping enhances the antibacterial activity of CQDs. This is consistent with other studies demonstrating the effectiveness of metal doping in improving the antibacterial activity of CQDs (Tejwan et al., 2021).

The microstructures of composite films have a significant impact on their mechanical properties. Fig. 2 a presents SEM images of the cross-sections of the PVA and composite films with varying concentrations of Zn-CQDs. However, it is challenging to prepare samples using liquid nitrogen quenching, owing to the hydrophilic nature of PVA. Consequently, samples can only be obtained through scissor cutting, resulting in irregular cross sections in the SEM images. The SEM images of the pure PVA film cross-section showed numerous small wrinkles, whereas the film incorporated with Zn-CQDs exhibited fewer small wrinkles, and no noticeable differences were observed between the films with different Zn-CQDs contents. Based on these outcomes and the mechanical performance, it is evident that the Zn-CQDs can be successfully incorporated into the matrix, possibly owing to hydrogen bonding between the Zn-CQDs and PVA.

The thickness of composite films with varying Zn-CQDs content is presented in Fig. 2b. The thinnest film was observed for pure PVA (52.24 ± 4.02 μm), while an increase in Zn-CQDs content resulted in an increase in film thickness. This can be attributed to the increased dry matter content of the composite film owing to the incorporation of Zn-CQDs, which is a common observation in various nanomaterial composite films. Riahi et al. also reported an increase in film thickness with an increasing amount of chitosan CQDs in composite films, which were made using carboxymethyl cellulose and chitosan CQDs (Riahi, Rhim, Bagheri, Pircheraghi, & Lotfali, 2022).

The mechanical properties of composite films, such as the tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EAB), serve to shield food from external pollutants and potential damage. The TS and EAB values of the composite films were used to assess their toughness and degree of stretching, respectively. The effect of the introduction of the Zn-CQDs on the TS and EAB of the composite films is illustrated in Fig. 2c. Compared to the films with lower Zn-CQDs content (0% and 1%), the TS of the 3% and 5% Zn-CQDs content of films were significantly improved (P < 0.05), and the EAB also increased significantly from 405.03 ± 40.01 and 412.60 ± 55.22 to 530.14 ± 79.50 and 514.84 ± 37.55 (P < 0.05). CQDs possess abundant functional groups and a small particle size, making them suitable candidates for enhancing the mechanical properties of composite films through either chemical or physical crosslinking mechanisms (Cuevas et al., 2019). The enhanced mechanical properties could be explained by the formation of hydrogen bonds between the Zn-CQDs and PVA. However, the decrease in the EAB of the 5% Zn-CQDs film could be attributed to the potential disruption of the PVA chain structure due to the high concentration of Zn-CQDs.

Color is an essential parameter that influences the purchase intention of consumers for films. The color of the composite film was expressed as the L value (lightness), a value (greenness/redness), and b value (blueness/yellowness) (H. Du et al., 2021). In addition, ΔE is a measure of the color difference between the film sample and standard white tile, which reflects the color intensity of the film. As shown in Table 1 , the L-value of the composite film decreased from 94.37 ± 0.14 (1% Zn-CQDs content) to 93.98 ± 0.20 (5% Zn-CQDs content), while the a-value decreased from −0.59 ± 0.05 (1% Zn-CQDs content) to −1.22 ± 0.04 (5% Zn-CQDs content), and the b-value increased from 3.43 ± 0.08 (1% Zn-CQDs content) to 5.94 ± 0.31 (5% Zn-CQDs content). In addition, ΔE increased with increasing Zn-CQDs concentration. All these values were significantly different (P < 0.05). These results indicate that the film became darker, greener, yellower, and brownish-yellow in color with increasing Zn-CQDs concentration. Compared to the standard whiteboard, the color of the film was also more intense, with an obvious color difference. The brown-yellow color of CQDs typically resulted in a color change of the composite films. Ezati et al. reported that pectin/gelatin-based composite films became darker (L-values decreased from 87.8 to 80.2) and yellower (b values increased from 17.7 to 29.4) with the addition of CQDs (Ezati, Roy, & Rhim, 2022).

Transparent packaging films are convenient for the observation and evaluation of food quality. However, these films allow light to pass through, which may negatively affect the quality of light-sensitive foods. Thus, it is essential for films to block certain wavelengths of light, such as ultraviolet light, to extend the shelf life of products. As shown in Fig. 2d-e, all the composite films and the PVA film exhibited high transmittance in the visible region, indicating good transparency. However, as the content of Zn-CQDs increased, the UV–Vis absorbance of the films increased and the transmittance decreased in the ultraviolet region. The transmittance of the film below 320 nm (the critical values of UVA and UVB) was 79.47% (PVA), 63.83% (1% Zn-CQDs content), 60.30% (3% Zn-CQDs content), and 52.59% (5% Zn-CQDs content), indicating that the composite film could potentially minimize food damage by ultraviolet light. Recently, CQD films have been used for UV absorption. For example, Hess et al. prepared UV-blocking films using PVA and CQDs from citric acid and polyethylene imine and achieved 90% UV absorbance (Hess et al., 2017). Patil et al. fabricated a PVA/waste tea residue CQDs film that successfully blocked 20–60% of UV-A (320–400 nm) (Patil et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Moisture content, water solubility, water vapor permeability and water contact angle
ce:section.ce:para: The moisture content and water solubility of the composite films are shown in Fig. 2f. The moisture content exhibited no significant changes with the variations in Zn-CQDs content (P > 0.05). However, the water solubility significantly increased (P < 0.05) as the Zn-CQDs content increased to 5%, potentially because of the hydrophilic nature of the Zn-CQDs.

The moisture content, water contact angle (WCA), and water vapor permeability (WVP) (Fig. 2g-h) are critical factors affecting the packaging film and directly impact moisture content (Tang et al., 2018). With the addition of Zn-CQDs, the angle decreased from 37.7° (PVA) to 32.6° (1% Zn-CQDs content), 30.6° (3% Zn-CQDs content) and 33.3° (5% Zn-CQDs content), but there was no significant difference (Fig. 2g, P > 0.05). The addition of Zn-CQDs increased the WVP of the composite film, which was significantly different from that of the pure PVA film (P < 0.05); however, there was no significant difference in all composite films (Fig. 2h, P > 0.05).

The reduction in water vapor barrier properties is a common drawback associated with the incorporation of CQDs into package films. Lin et al. prepared N-doped CQDs/chitosan package film, resulting in increased solubility and WVP with the addition of more CQDs. The pH of the environment also plays a significant role in determining the WVP. In an acidic environment, the network structure of the film matrix may be disrupted, resulting in increased WVP (W. Lin et al., 2022). Similarly, Purkayastha et al. reported that CQDs/rapeseed protein films showed an increased WVP (P. M. Das et al., 2014). The hydrophilicity of CQDs and the formation of hydrogen bonds between the CQDs and the polymer matrix are the reasons for this phenomena (Khoshkalampour, Ghorbani, & Ghasempour, 2023).

Thermal analysis of the composite films was performed using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). As shown in Fig. 2i, an initial significant thermal degradation was observed at 30–160 °C, which may be attributed to the loss of moisture in the composite film. The second degradation occurred at 160–380 °C, which may be ascribed to the degradation of the polymer skeleton structure of the composite film and thermal decomposition of PVA. At this stage, the curve of the composite film moved downward with decreasing Zn-CQDs content, indicating that the addition of Zn-CQDs slowed the thermal decomposition of the film. The third significant thermal decomposition occurred at about 380–460 °C, which could potentially arise from the degradation of the carbon-based structure and the byproducts generated by the thermal decomposition of PVA. The weight loss rates of the thin films at 380 °C were 85.85% (PVA), 76.65% (1% Zn-CQDs content), 73.22% (3% Zn-CQDs content) and 72.2% (5% Zn-CQDs content). These finding indicate that the incorporation of Zn-CQDs enhances the thermal stability of the composite films. Similar findings have been reported in other studies of CQDs films. For instance, Zhao et al. prepared a CQDs/PVA composite film using banana CQDs and PVA, and conducted thermogravimetric analysis, The findings revealed that the incorporation of Zn-CQDs has a positive impact on the thermal stability of the composite films (Zhang et al., 2022).

FT-IR spectroscopy was used to analyze the surface functional groups present in the Zn-CQDs (Fig. 2j). The peaks at 3394 cm−1 and 2963 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of O-H/N-H and C-H, respectively (Chellasamy, Arumugasamy, Govindaraju, & Yun, 2022). The strong peak at 1577 cm−1 was associated with the stretching vibration of C=O, while the peak observed at 1416 cm−1 was linked to the stretching vibration of C-N (Gu et al., 2021; He & Du, 2023). The N functional groups indicated that the nitrogen from the crayfish shell was doped onto the Zn-CQDs. Additionally, the peak at 1023 cm−1 corresponds to C-O-C (Hongying Du, He, Zhang, Manyande, & Chen, 2023). The abundant hydrophilic groups contributed to the excellent water solubility of the Zn-CQDs, as previously reported in various studies (Y. Li et al., 2021; R. Zhou et al., 2022). FTIR spectroscopy was also employed to investigate the interaction between the Zn-CQDs and PVA. The spectra of Zn-CQDs-PVA composite films and PVA films were very similar, which can be described by the PVA peaks covering the peaks of small amounts of Zn-CQDs. Notably, the peak corresponding to the O-H stretching vibrations of Zn-CQDs at 3394 cm−1 shifted to 3270-3264 cm−1, which is a common interaction between CQDs and the matrix. For example, Khoshkalampour et al. prepared a CQDs/gelatin composite film, and the hydrogen bonding between the CQDs and the gelatin resulted in the movement of the -OH peak in FTIR (Khoshkalampour et al., 2023).

Antioxidant activity is a critical aspect of food packaging films as it plays a significant role in maintaining food quality. The antioxidant activity of three films was assessed by measuring their ability to scavenge DPPH and ABTS free radicals (Fig. 2k). The PVA film exhibited a weak scavenging effect on free radicals. As the concentration of Zn-CQDs in the composite films increased, the DPPH scavenging ability gradually improved, whereas the ABTS scavenging ability increased sharply. The composite film with 5% Zn-CQDs demonstrated the highest scavenging ability, with rates of 67.62% for DPPH and 94.94% for ABTS, which were significantly different from those of the PVA film (P < 0.05). The difference in scavenging activity between DPPH and ABTS is mainly due to the hydrophilicity of the films. The PVA film was more readily soluble in water (the solvent for ABTS) than in ethanol (the solvent for DPPH), allowing the CQDs to be released more rapidly in the ABTS aqueous solution.

In this experiment, based on various properties and antioxidant property, 5% concentration of Zn-CQDs was selected to preserve the mangoes. The appearance, incidence of disease, hardness, VC content, titratable acidity, total soluble solids, and weight loss of the control, PVA, and composite film groups were measured and compared every two days during the 10-day storage period. The aim of this experiment was to determine the effectiveness of a composite film in enhancing the shelf life of mangoes.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, the appearance of mangoes varied among different storage groups. In the control group, the appearance of mangoes rapidly deteriorated because of natural decay at room temperature. Within 2–4 days, browning areas appeared on the surface, and black spots began to spread quickly from the 6th day, ultimately covering over 90% of the fruit surface on the 10th day. Mangoes protected the PVA film also showed browning areas within 2–4 days, although the area was smaller than that in the control group. On the 6th day, black spots emerged, and on the 8th and 10th day, the browning and black spot areas expanded rapidly. However, the overall appearance of mangoes was better than that of the control group. Mangoes protected by the Zn-CQDs/PVA composite film exhibited no significant differences in appearance compared to fresh mangoes on the 2nd-6th day. On the 8th day, a small browning area appeared, which expanded on the 10th day, along with the appearance of black spots. At the end of the storage period, mangoes preserved by the CQDs/PVA composite film displayed a better appearance than those of the other groups, indicating that the composite film has great potential for maintaining mango quality.

The disease index (DI) of mangoes is a critical indicator to measure their commercial and edible value. A higher DI can lead to a decline in consumer purchase intentions and an increase in the possibility of foodborne diseases. As shown in Fig. 3b, the DI of the mangoes in the three groups increased during storage, albeit at different rates. On the second day of storage, the DI in the control group reached 0.543, while the DI in the PVA and composite film groups were 0.2 and 0.127, respectively. The difference in the disease index among the three groups was statistically significant (P < 0.05). On the 10th day, the DI of mangoes in the control group was 0.993, indicating that the mangoes in this group were completely decayed. The DI of the PVA group was 0.856 and that of the composite film group was 0.764, with a statistically significant difference (P < 0.05).

During storage, mangoes can decay and deteriorate due to aging, microbial invasion, and pathological changes. The application of the Zn-CQDs/PVA film on mangoes protects them from external physical damage and reduces the contact between mango fruit and external pollutants and microorganisms. Furthermore, the Zn-CQDs composite possesses antibacterial activity, which can slow down plant stress resistance and decrease the disease index of mango fruit.

Hardness is an important indicator of fruit quality and reflects the overall state of the fruit. As illustrated in Fig. 3c, the hardness of mango fruits decreased as the storage time increased. A significant difference in fruit hardness was detected between treatment groups and control group on the 4th day (P < 0.05). On the 10th day of storage, the maximum hardness of mango fruit was observed in the composite film group (4.18 N), whereas the minimum hardness was observed in the control group (2.65 N). The PVA film exhibited an intermediate hardness of 3.66 N. These results suggest that the composite film was effective in preventing the decline in fruit hardness. The composite film acted as a physical barrier, leading to a reduction in the respiration rate of the mango fruit and the inhibition of hydrolytic enzyme activity, thereby slowing down the process of mango softening and spoilage (Hassan, Chatha, Hussain, Zia, & Akhtar, 2018).

Vitamin C (VC) is an essential water-soluble vitamin of utmost importance to human health. It is abundant in mangoes and is used to evaluate the nutritional quality and ripeness of fruit. The VC content in mangoes decreased over time during storage (Fig. 3d), with significant differences (P < 0.05) observed among the three treatment groups on the second day of storage. On the 10th day of storage, the control group experienced a nearly 50% reduction in the VC content of mango to 30.2 mg/g. Meanwhile, the VC content of mango in the PVA and composite film groups was 37.5 mg/g and 38.5 mg/g, respectively, indicating that the coating treatment slowed the depletion of VC content in mangos by providing a low-oxygen environment that reduced the external oxygen exposure as well as oxidation of VC (Ebrahimi & Rastegar, 2020).

Acid is a natural preservative in mango fruits, contributing to the flavor and resistance to the invasion of external microorganisms. Thus, the titratable acidity is an essential indicator for evaluating mango maturity. Organic acids are metabolized through processes such as mango respiration and the tricarboxylic acid cycle, which ultimately leads to the conversion of these acids into carbon dioxide and water (X. Fan, Sokorai, Engemann, Gurtler, & Liu, 2012). As depicted in Fig. 3e, the titratable acid content in the mango fruits of all groups exhibited a declining trend during storage. Notably, the control group displayed the most rapid decline, exhibiting a significant difference compared to the mango in all other groups by the second day (P < 0.05). By the tenth day of storage, the titratable acidity of mango fruits in the control group was nearly entirely depleted, whereas those treated with the composite film retained a relatively elevated titratable acid content. This can be attributed to the reduced respiration rate of mangoes and the removal of reactive oxygen species by the antibacterial properties of the composite film, which helps mangoes resist the adverse effects of microbial invasion.

Total soluble solids are vital indicators for assessing the maturity and taste of mango fruits. As depicted in Fig. 3f, the soluble solid content of mango fruits in all groups increased during storage. On the second day of storage, mango fruits in the control group displayed a rapid increase in soluble solid content, whereas those treated with PVA and the composite films showed a more gradual rise, with a significant difference among the three groups (P < 0.05). The respiration rate continued to increase during storage, leading to moisture evaporation. Furthermore, the conversion of starch and other macromolecular polysaccharides into monosaccharides contributed to the accumulation in total soluble solids. On the 10th day of storage, the soluble solid content of mangoes in the control group decreased, which may have been caused by the consumption of sugar as a substrate for respiration and microorganisms.

Previous research has demonstrated an upward trend of soluble solids in mango fruit during storage, followed by a decline, which aligns with the results of this study. However, an initial upward trend, followed by a downward trend, has been reported in some studies. The difference between these cases may be attributed to various factors such as storage conditions, experimental period, mango varieties, and coating materials. For instance, Yang et al. developed chitosan/carboxymethyl/mustard essential oil amphiphilic films to preserve mango fruit and observed that the soluble solid content of the mango fruit increased during storage period, albeit at slower rate (Z. Yang et al., 2023). In contrast, Xu et al. used a fucoidan coating to preserve mango fruits and observed an initial increase followed by a decline in the soluble solids of mangoes, with the control group reaching peak soluble solids earlier than the treatment group (B. Xu & Wu, 2021).

The quality of mangoes can be assessed based on their weight loss, which is primarily the result of respiratory processes and moisture evaporation. As depicted in Fig. 3g, the weight loss rate of all groups demonstrated a gradual and continuous increase over the storage. After 10 d of storage, the weight loss rates of mangoes in the composite film group were 10.1%, the PVA group was 10.9%, and the control group was 13.6%, respectively. Notably, significantly higher weight loss rates were observed in the two treatment groups than in the control group (P < 0.05), indicating that the treated mangoes retained their weight more effectively than those in the control group. This can be attributed to the fact that the film surrounding the fruit acts as a barrier, reducing the moisture loss from the mango.

A Zn-CQDs/PVA composite film was prepared by PVA and Zn-CQDs, which were synthesized using zinc acetate and crayfish shells as precursors. The incorporation of Zn-CQDs into the PVA matrix improved the mechanical properties, ultraviolet–visible absorption, and thermal stability of the film. The mango preservation experiment demonstrated that the composite film could delay the decline in hardness, VC content, and titratable acidity, as well as an increase in disease index, total soluble solids, and weight loss rate of mangoes during storage. These results indicate that the Zn-CQDs/PVA composite has the potential for use as an active packaging film to maintain the quality and prolong the shelf life of mangoes. Thus, the current Zn-CQDs/PVA composite film shows promise for future applications in the food industry as a novel active food-packaging material.

Hongying Du: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Linjing He: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Jin Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Hong-Xiu Lu: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
